title: PID reuse: worker liveness must be pid+starttime, not pid alone
description: HIGH hazard. A dead worker's pid gets recycled by the OS for an unrelated process, so pid_alive returns True. Two consequences: the sweep never reclaims the slot (phantom worker wedges a slot - observed pid 86881 recycled into a macOS CascadeSets system process, 1 of 3 agents stuck), and worse, sweep.kill sends SIGTERM/SIGKILL to whatever now owns that pid (we killed a system process demonstrating it). Fix: identify a worker by pid + process start-time, or verify argv contains the spawnid/worker_session. Record start-time at spawn; check it so a recycled pid reads dead and kill refuses a mismatched pid.

title: Pause/resume a killed agent with its context (session --resume) for token efficiency
description: When a worker is killed for OUR reasons - graceful tg run shutdown (SIGINT) or the circuit breaker (GRID-058) cooling on a rate/session limit - relaunch its Claude Code session with --resume instead of a blank agent, so it keeps its reasoning (re-reading/re-planning/what-it-tried) and we don't re-pay those tokens. Mechanism exists: Claude sessions are resumable; the GRID-069 runner already drives a session and just needs to record task->session-id in the registry and relaunch with --resume. Resume ONLY on intentional kills; orphan/crash kills stay reclaim-and-restart (a wedged agent's context is corrupt). Caveat: resume is for SHORT pauses (breaker cooldown, quick restart) - a long pause carries stale context that diverges from a rebased worktree, so fall back to a fresh commit-preserved start. Complements commit-incrementally (work survives) by making context cheap to continue; pairs with GRID-058 + GRID-069.

title: Priority on epics/stories/tasks feeding the claim ordering
description: Runner should select ready tasks by priority so bug-fixes/test-improvements jump the queue. Design: priority lives on the EPIC (GRID-072's mandatory container, the unit of urgency) and flows down to stories/tasks; tg epic --priority high; claim_ready orders by priority then FIFO within a priority (tiebreak prevents starvation-chaos). Add aging (effective priority creeps up with wait time) only if starvation appears. It's a claim-time sort, NOT preemption - changes what's picked next, never kills in-flight agents. Agnostic-clean (generic work-management, no step/role names).

title: build: GRID-058-session-limit-circuit-breaker
notes:
from review (rejected): Full suite is NOT green: bash tests/run.sh failed on tests/integration/test_tg.py::TestRun::test_run_spawns_when_prior_worker_already_claimed (1 failed, 708 passed, 806s). It passes alone and in the isolated file (129 passed) but fails under the full suite - a branch-introduced flake, not a pre-existing one. Production code (parser, Breaker domain, breaker_gate, adapter, tick gate, restart-safe state) is correct and matches the spec; the defect is confined to the test changes in tests/integration/test_tg.py. Root cause: the assertions were changed from len(self._workers()) (counts workers.json entries, reaping-immune) to len(self._alive_workers()) (counts pids where os.kill(pid,0) succeeds). TestRun spawns via GRID_SPAWN_CMD='echo x >> {log}', which exits instantly; the spawner Popen is never waited, so the new worker is only 'alive' as an unreaped zombie. os.kill(pid,0) returns True for a zombie, so _alive_workers() briefly counts it as the 2nd 'alive' worker. Under the full suite's subprocess churn the zombie gets reaped before the assertion, so the count drops to 1 -> failure. Verified the mechanism directly: pid_alive is True for a zombie and False after os.waitpid reaps it. Fix: don't assert liveness of the throwaway spawned worker. Assert what the test actually cares about deterministically - the prior/claimed worker's own pid is still alive (proving it wasn't swept), AND a new worker was appended (len(self._workers()) == 2). test_run_spawns_when_inflight_worker_is_stale (line 574) has the identical latent flake (also uses _alive_workers()==2) and should get the same treatment. Re-run the full bash tests/run.sh (not just the file) to confirm green before done.
outcome: done

title: Planning agent - decompose a LOCKED spec into filed tasks/stories + wire dependencies (blocked-by / blast-radius)
description: After a spec is reviewed and LOCKED (post human review-plan), a planning agent takes it and produces the filed stories/tasks with correct dependencies: (a) intra-spec phase ordering (--blocked-by between phases), and (b) cross-spec BLAST-RADIUS - if the spec touches the same files/subsystem as other in-flight/planned work, or references another spec, set --blocked-by accordingly. This is the step that would have caught the olt/GRID-058 miss (GRID-058 reuses c1y/GRID-053's kill path -> should have been blocked-by GRID-053; filed in parallel -> semantic conflict). Distinct from lru (spec pre-review = QUALITY, before the human) and the spec-writer agent (DRAFTING). Pipeline: seed -> spec-writer -> lru(quality) -> human approve/LOCK -> PLANNING(decompose + deps) -> file. NOTE the tension: the-grid removed a broad planner (3z2 retired; driver decomposes inline) - this is a NARROWER, dependency-focused decomposer on a locked spec, worth reconsidering distinctly. Interim (until it exists): the driver does the blast-radius/blocked-by check by hand at file time. Ties: lru, spec-writer agent, dg2 lineage, GRID-043 resolver (prevent vs resolve).

title: convention: fast-path verification for step-file/docs-only diffs
description:
Recurring friction (3+ epics: afo.2, the-grid-9mr.2, the-grid-j2l.14): a step-file or CLAUDE.md-only change still requires the full subprocess integration/feature suite (5.5-9+ min) to verify, because there is no cheap check that a prose-only diff can't regress code behaviour. afo.2: "full suite runs ~6.5min for a docs-only markdown edit... no cheap way to gate a pure step-file change short of the whole run." the-grid-9mr.2: "full suite is ~9m15s... a brief-only change still costs a long green-verify - the fast unit subset covers nothing about this markdown." the-grid-j2l.14: "full suite is slow (~5.5 min)... had to re-run in foreground to confirm 551 passed" after a background run buffered empty output.
Recommendation: add a lightweight diff-shape check (e.g. to the reviewer/verify step or CLAUDE.md Tests convention) that recognises a diff touching only steps/*.md and/or CLAUDE.md, and permits verification via lint + the fast unit subset alone, reserving the full integration/feature run for diffs that touch the-grid/ source. Document the convention so reviewers stop paying the full suite tax on pure-prose changes.

title: Spec-writer agent - draft a spec from a seed, paired with spec pre-review (lru)
description: An agent that DRAFTS a spec from a backlog seed (vs today's driver+human co-design in develop). Pairs with lru (spec pre-review, which vets the draft): seed -> spec-writer -> spec pre-review (lru) -> human review-plan -> tg file. Shifts the human from co-author to approver, automating more of the develop phase. The real design question: how much of spec authoring to automate - authoring may be the highest-value thing to keep human (intent), or the highest-value to offload (mechanics). Ties to lru, the driver's 'protect the human's attention' purpose, the removed planner, and the workflow-flexibility theme (hsj/kzu/lp0). Capture for the design pass, not a quick spec.

title: build: GRID-048-id-prefix-from-bd-not-dirname
notes:
from review (rejected): The prefix probe is not universally non-mutating and breaks on the-grid's own store. _discover_prefix probes with a hardcoded id 'the-grid-prefix-discovery-probe-0' and assumes bd will REJECT it (id/prefix mismatch) so it can scrape the real prefix from the error. But bd accepts any --id that starts with '<prefix>-'. On a grid whose bd prefix is 'the-grid' (the natural prefix for a root named 'the-grid' - i.e. this very project), bd CREATES the bead (rc=0). _discover_prefix then hits the 'error is None' branch and raises SystemExit, so every tg command crashes AND leaves a stray bead 'the-grid-prefix-discovery-probe-0' polluting the live store. Verified by hand: 'bd init' in a temp dir named 'the-grid', then the exact probe command -> rc=0, bead created. The test suite is green only because no test uses a root whose prefix is a prefix-string of the probe id (foo-1_2 doesn't collide). This violates the spec's explicit 'non-mutating' requirement. Fix: source the prefix without a create that can succeed - prefer a read-only bd command (bd config/context/info exposing the canonical issue prefix), or read it back from an existing bead's id, or make the probe id one bd cannot possibly accept for ANY prefix (and still guard the create-succeeded branch by deleting the stray bead before raising). Add a test with a 'the-grid'-named (prefix-colliding) root so this case is covered.
outcome: done

title: tg doctor/fsck + safe tg delete for live-store repair
description: Diagnosing a live tg failure (e.g. an orphaned partial story) REQUIRES inspecting the live grid store - but CLAUDE.md says never touch the live store, and there is no documented safe repair path, so removing an orphan means raw bd against live (which the rules forbid). Need: tg doctor/tg fsck (find malformed stories, orphaned tasks, missing artifacts) and a safe tg delete <id> that snapshots-backup first. Ties to ge0 (backup) and live-store hygiene. From portability report.

title: resolve .beads/issues.jsonl gitignored vs durable-snapshot intent
description: The methodology treats the exported bd snapshot (.beads/issues.jsonl) as the durable measurement substrate that must survive ('back up before restructure', 'export + commit'). But that path is gitignored, so the auto-export's 'git add .beads/issues.jsonl' hard-fails. Intent and ignore rule are in direct conflict. Decide which is authoritative: either the snapshot must be committable (un-gitignore it / git add -f), or the auto-export must be a no-op when the path is intentionally ignored rather than a hard failure. Ties ge0. From portability report.

title: nested repos are filable but invisible in _available_repos
description: _available_repos (file_story.py) lists only DIRECT git-repo children of the projects root. Repos nested one level deeper (e.g. under a grouping dir like high-school-science/) are NOT listed in the available-repos error, yet ARE accepted if you pass the nested path (validation only checks is_git_repo(projects_root/<repo>)). Discovery and validation disagree - a valid filable repo is reported as non-existent. Fix: walk one+ levels deep when listing (bounded, stop at first git repo per branch), or a config repo registry/glob; at minimum the error message should acknowledge nested paths work. From portability report.

title: file_story is non-atomic - a mid-op failure orphans a partial story (no spec/task)
description: FileStoryUseCase.execute does create_story -> add_artifact(spec) -> add_artifact(repo) -> create_task -> dep_add with NO transaction/rollback. If a later step fails (e.g. add_artifact calls bd show which failed under Bug 1's broken qualify), the story bead is committed but artifacts/task are not -> an inert orphan (no spec = pool can't build; no task = no flow). Fixes: (a) make filing atomic or roll back the created story on failure (delete the bead, or defer story creation until artifacts+task ready); (b) tg status/tg backlog must distinguish a malformed story (no spec/no child task) from a healthy queued one - a corrupt half-story currently reads as healthy. Also: the auto-export step failed because it tried 'git add .beads/issues.jsonl' which is gitignored (see separate snapshot-vs-gitignore item). From portability report (dotted-root machine).

title: tg run has no singleton guard - multiple instances can run at once (no pidfile/lock)
description: cmd_run is just while True: tick() with no pidfile, flock, or running-instance check. Nothing stops two tg run loops ticking simultaneously (easy to hit given the pull+restart dance - an old instance survives if not Ctrl-C'd). Consequences: (1) each instance enforces max_agents INDEPENDENTLY, so N instances = N x max agents (at max-agents=10, two loops = 20 agents); (2) double PR-monitoring and double cadence-gate firing (duplicate resolvers/audits); (3) concurrent bd writers = the ejb single-writer contention, racing/corrupting the store. Fix: acquire an exclusive lock at tg run start (pidfile with flock, or a lock row in the store) naming the holder; on a second start, refuse with a clear message ('tg run already running, pid N') or take over after verifying the old pid is dead. Stale-lock recovery: if the pid in the file is not alive, reclaim. Ties: ejb (bd contention), tp1 (restart discipline), i5x. Given max-agents just raised to 10, this is now higher-risk.

title: Self-hosting sync gap: loop runs a local checkout that goes stale vs origin after merges - merged engine improvements are inert until manual git pull + restart
description: the-grid improves its own engine via PRs merged to origin/main, but tg run executes the LOCAL grid-root checkout. After merging on GitHub, local main is behind until someone git pulls - so a merged engine change (e.g. the GRID-043 resolver) is physically absent from the running code even after a tg run restart. Observed live: merged #96 (resolver), restarted tg run, resolver still didn't fire because local main was still at ac209b1 (session start) - never pulled all session; origin/main was 30 files / e4dda11 ahead. M1 only kept working because merge->close shipped before the session. Extends i5x (stale in-process code) with the repo-level variant (stale checkout vs origin). Also implicates the build base: pool worktrees branched off stale local main all session, which is a root contributor to the conflict cascade. Fix options: (a) a sync step/discipline - after merging an engine PR, git pull --ff-only + restart; (b) the loop fetch+ff-pulls origin/main each tick (or on a cadence) and self-restarts on change; (c) run the loop from a checkout that tracks origin. Ties: i5x. Core to the self-hosting story.

title: build: GRID-043-merge-conflict-resolver
notes:
from review (rejected): Conflict detection never fires on the real ready-merge step. In monitor_prs.py the branches are an if/elif chain ending in 'elif rework_outcome: ... elif conflict_outcome and is_conflicted(...)'. The real steps/ready-merge.md declares BOTH on_pr_rework: changes AND on_pr_conflict: conflicted, so rework_outcome ('changes') is always truthy for a ready-merge task; the 'elif rework_outcome' branch is entered for every open PR, does nothing when there's no /rework comment, and the conflict branch is unreachable. Result: the exact scenario the spec targets (a ready-merge task whose PR goes conflicting after a sibling merges) never routes to resolve. title: 2b nags: an incrementally-built epic shows as a close-candidate in every lull between stories
description: 2b flags an epic as a close-candidate whenever all its children are closed. But an epic built one story at a time has a lull after each story closes and before the next is filed - and a long design/planning gap (e.g. 707 during the story-3 design) leaves it flagged 'for quite some time' while clearly still in progress. 'No open children' conflates 'done' with 'between stories / mid-planning'; the engine can't see planned-but-unfiled work. The human-gate resolution (surface it, human judges) becomes persistent nagging. Options: (a) quiescence gate - only flag after all-closed AND untouched for a meaningful window, distinguishing 'forgotten' from 'between stories'; (b) planned-scope awareness - link the design spec's remaining stories to the epic (dg2 lineage) so it is not a candidate until all planned work is done; (c) explicit in-progress state cleared by the human. This is a process defect the auditor should have caught.

title: Measure auditor/analyst judgement quality by recommendation-acceptance rate
description: The value of the auditor (2d) and the story-3 trend analyst is the quality of their judgement, which has no ground truth to unit-test - so GRID-033/034 leave it 'validated by use'. But the feedback loop can validate it: track what fraction of auditor/analyst findings the human triages INTO work vs dismisses. A persistent low acceptance rate means the role is generating noise - itself a process defect the auditor/analyst should surface. Trend it as the one metric that measures the system's judgement about itself, closing the loop on its own quality. Sketch: tag auditor/analyst-origin items; record disposition (promoted-to-work vs dismissed); expose acceptance rate as a signal. Follow-up to the feedback-loop epic 707.

Reference the epic via task METADATA, not parent; the auditor reads the epic id from metadata. (3) TERMINAL CLOSE - auditor.md does 'tg done TASK done' but the audit step is routeless, so tg done raises 'no transition'. Make a routeless (terminal) step close on tg done (a step with routes still errors on an unknown outcome - typo protection); this also makes cleanup closeable. Keep the agnostic + terminal-close covered by unit tests.

title: tg run loop executes stale engine code until manually restarted
description: The pool loop (tg run) runs the code it was started with; merging engine improvements (M1/M2/M3, remote-branch cleanup, epic-close, ...) has no effect in the running loop until it is manually restarted. As the-grid self-hosts and self-improves, each improvement is inert until restart, and it is easy to believe a feature is live when it is not - e.g. GRID-028's remote-branch deletion appeared to work on its own merge, but the branches were actually deleted by hand because the loop had not been restarted. Options: the loop detects the engine repo HEAD moving and restarts/reloads itself, or surfaces a 'restart needed' signal in tg status/ps. Ties to self-update. Also affects M-series behaviour: features only take effect one restart after they land.

title: build: GRID-025-m1-merge-triggers-cleanup
notes:
from review (rejected): Agnostic-rule violation (hard reject, overrides the spec's wording). MonitorMergedPrsUseCase hardcodes the workflow step literal in the engine: 'if task.step != "ready-merge"' (the_grid/application/pool/monitor_merged_prs.py:31). 'ready-merge' is a step defined only in the editable workflow markdown (steps/ready-merge.md); baking it into the engine's tick loop couples the engine to this one workflow - CLAUDE.md's canonical violation ('hardcode a workflow step name, e.g. build'). No other engine code hardcodes a literal step name. A frontend/data-pipeline workflow that renames or drops that step would silently break the monitor. Everything else is sound: 427 tests pass, ruff clean, GitHubEventsPort/adapter/fake + wiring + CloseStory teardown all correct; keep them. Fix = derive the watch set without the literal. Two viable directions: (a) select open tasks whose parent story carries a 'pr' artifact (the pr-artifact presence is the agnostic watch signal - 'pr' already has engine precedent in block_task/worklog) and let the flow drive the outcome; or (b) ask the flow which step(s) route on the merge outcome (Flow.outcomes_for / the routes) instead of a hardcoded name, so the editable steps stay the source of truth. Watch for over-match under (a): a story may carry a pr before it reaches the merge gate - derive the gate from the flow, don't assume every pr-bearing story should close on merge.
outcome: done

title: Ubiquitous-language pass: settle the domain vocabulary and evict bd's 'bead' term
notes:
Raised by TRON during GRID-014. 'bead' is bd's storage primitive, NOT this system's domain language. After GRID-014's structural refactor settles, do a dedicated ubiquitous-language pass: (1) DECIDE the canonical domain vocabulary (Task/Story/Artifact/Flow/Step/Role/Outcome/Transition/Worker/Worktree/Reflection/Signal/Retro/Worklog/...); (2) evict 'bead' everywhere it is NOT the anti-corruption boundary. 'bead' is correct ONLY in adapters/bead.py (the bd wire-format translator). Leak sites to fix: StorePort.ensure_beads/list_beads_by_status (port should speak domain terms); FakeStore self._beads/_ready_bead_dicts/_new_bead; test names (test_fake_store 'bead' asserts, store_contract); the bd lifecycle status vocabulary (open/in_progress/closed) surfaced via history(); retro's bd-history read. Gate: do this AFTER GRID-014 (the-grid-cmi) so names settle once, not repeatedly.
PULLED FORWARD (TRON spot-check found 'bead' still leaking into worker/pool/store as MISSING ABSTRACTIONS, not just naming). DECISIONS: (1) Worker.bead -> Worker.task (the task a worker runs); WorkersPort.stamp_bead -> set_task; spawner/workers.json use 'task'. (2) claim-ownership: bead_to_task populates Task.claimed_by (bd assignee=spawnid); store.claimed_tasks() -> [Task] replaces list_beads_by_status; store.reclaim(tid); sweep reads t.claimed_by. (3) StorePort.ensure_beads -> ensure_store. BATCH A = Worker.task rename; BATCH B = Task.claimed_by + claimed_tasks + reclaim + ensure_store + sweep.
batch A DONE -> PR #63 (lang-worker-task): Worker.bead->Worker.task; WorkersPort.stamp_bead->set_task; spawner/workers.json 'task'; consumers (claim_task/resolve_log/trace/tick/cli ps 'task='). batch B DONE -> PR #64 (lang-claimed-by): Task.claimed_by (from assignee); list_beads_by_status->claimed_tasks (typed); StorePort.reclaim; sweep de-beaded; ensure_beads->ensure_store; FakeStore _beads->_records; retro bead_id->subject_id; artifact docstring. title: Domain OO/DDD remediation: full object-oriented model + typed use-case I/O
notes:
GRID-014. Successor to GRID-013 (the-grid-1b8). Design doc: specs/domain-oo-ddd-remediation.md (local, gitignored - bd is canonical).

WHY: GRID-013 laid the hexagonal layering down but the domain it produced is anemic and leaky. backend-architecture-review + spot-checks agreed: (1) domain is module-level functions over dicts, not a model; (2) bd wire format leaks into the domain (Task.from_bead, label_value/_status_of, ready_roles_from_beads, derive_signals' h["Issue"]["status"]); (3) hardcoded workflow vocab ("build"/"review"/"open-pr") in retro.py = agnostic-rule breach + primitive obsession; (4) worklog.py:28 dt.astimezone() reads ambient OS tz = purity breach + latent bug; (5) analyze_flow CC~25, render_log_line CC~18-28; (6) 25 of 26 use cases return untyped dicts/primitives.

GOAL: reshape domain/ into a strict, fully OO DDD model that reads as a clean reference model - transposable to Kotlin/Go for a binary.

DECISIONS (settled):
1. Reference model for a port - clarity/transposition over Python ergonomics.
2. No bare functions, no helpers in domain/ - full OO.
3. Six fine-grained subdomains: flow, contracts, work, pool, workspace, feedback.
4. Central ports/ kept, but typed (return domain objects, not dicts). NOT collocated in subdomains.
5. BdStore is the anti-corruption mapper - all bd wire format moves to the adapter.
6. application/ mirrors the six contexts + thin setup/ (InitGrid). inspect/intake dissolve into context.
7. Use cases: XUseCase + typed XInput/XResponse (frozen dataclasses, collocated). CLI renders off Response.
8. Status stays a StrEnum (transposes to Kotlin enum class).
9. Three modules leave domain: steps.py -> FsAdapter; flow.compose_driver -> app/adapter; logrender.py -> cli.
10. Behaviour-preserving, phased like GRID-013. tg output byte-identical; feature/integration suites are the net.

TARGET MODEL:
- flow/: Flow(agg root), Step, Role, Outcome, Transition. Flow.assemble(roles); next(step,outcome); owner_of; ready_roles; next_task_spec.
- contracts/: ArtifactRequirement, StepContract (missing_inputs/required_outputs), FlowContracts(flow) replacing analyze_flow god-fn.
- work/: Task (rich entity), Story (agg w/ Artifacts), Status (StrEnum), Artifact, TaskQueue (bucket/mine/by_status) replacing tasks.py.
- pool/: Worker, WorkerPool, PoolPlan.decide.
- workspace/: Worktree (branch_name/path_in/is_lock_error); IO stays in service/adapter; .gitignore via FsPort.
- feedback/: Reflection, Signal (typed history + Flow, no hardcoded steps), Retro, Worklog (tz injected).

PHASES (each its own PR set + review checkpoint, suite green throughout):
- Phase 1 domain objects: six contexts as rich objects; bead-format -> BdStore; evict steps.py/compose_driver/logrender; ports typed.
- Phase 2 use-case I/O: XUseCase + typed XInput/XResponse; application/ -> six contexts; CLI renders off Responses.
- Phase 3 de-anemic: push business logic from use cases down onto domain objects.
- Phase 4 testing: per-object unit tests; grow feature/ gherkin per-capability over FakeStore/FakeFs; thin integration tier; measure speedup.

RISKS: Phase 1 large (bead-format migration touches every read path - red suite = stop). Add a grep gate (bd-format keys + hardcoded step strings in domain/). Pin CLI render with feature suite before Phase 2.

title: Scope boundary: tg is the hub + planning assistant, not a universal agentic-dev solver
notes:
Refined vision: tg automates the toil that leaches focus (orchestration, state, who-needs-what, where-things-are) and assists planning. It does NOT try to solve every agentic-dev problem. Work with a better natural surface happens THERE, and tg links out + pulls feedback back:
- Spec WRITING: external (vanilla claude / superpowers skills) - experiment showed they beat an in-tg format.
- Spec/PR REVIEW: external, on the artifact's natural surface (GitHub comments, editor), with a feedback mechanism that pulls comments back into tg (this is 33j). tg does not render a review UI.
Consequences: the in-tg review-plan gate goes away; develop -> spec -> review-on-its-surface (comments) -> watcher pulls feedback -> build. The cockpit (gcc) is the hub end: shows state, links OUT to PRs/specs where review happens. Aligns with the agnostic rule. Reshapes 3z2 (planning streamlining) and elevates 33j from a feature to a scope boundary.
close_reason: encoded in METHODOLOGY.md ('What tg is - and isn't'). Downstream: 3z2 (review = link-out + feedback, not a gate), 33j (the feedback mechanism).

title: Foundational architecture refactor: full hexagonal + DDD + SOLID
notes:
Target structure (replaces core/adapters/cli):
- adapters/  - all IO implementations
- ports/     - the interfaces adapters implement (and the app depends on)
- application/ - use cases (ONE action per use case, grouped by activity/domain into folders) + cross-cutting services
- domain/    - DDD with strategic patterns done properly; NO IO in the domain layer

Cross-cutting principles:
- Full OOP + Python types throughout; SOLID.
- A DI container that manages and injects dependencies.
- A config object that loads external config files + env vars, FAILS FAST when absent - NO hidden default values.
- An init script that sets up the host system.

Tests:
- test/support/ - test adapters + the test container
- test/unit/, test/integration/
- test/feature/ - gherkin scenarios exercising the app via features

Approach: a SERIES OF EXTRACTIONS AND REFACTORS, not big-bang. This is the foundation for everything (incl testability).

Connections: B4's StorePort + FakeStore are already a port + a test adapter (down-payment). Supersedes the current CLAUDE.md 'hexagonal-lite/stdlib-only' conventions (those will be rewritten for this structure). Note: 'full OOP + types + SOLID' may revisit the current stdlib-only rule. Interacts with 3z2 (planning simplification) and 707 (feedback).
close_reason: GRID-013 delivered: all 6 phases merged (PRs #17-#40). Hexagonal/DDD refactor complete - domain entities+VOs, ports, application use cases, config-driven container, thin cli, four-tier test suite + transportable gherkin.

title: Agents load the wrong CLAUDE.md for multi-project (spawn cwd=grid_root, not the target repo)
notes:
Spawner sets cwd=grid_root, so claude -p loads the-grid's CLAUDE.md (+ workspace + global), NOT the target repo's - the agent only cd's into the worktree AFTER startup, and claude -p doesn't re-scan memory after a cd. Works only because we dogfood (the-grid building the-grid), where the loaded CLAUDE.md happens to be the target's.

Breaks for other target repos: the agent gets the-grid's conventions (hexagonal/stdlib/no-pytest) and never loads projects/<repo>/CLAUDE.md.

Root cause: spawn happens before claim, so the spawner doesn't know the target worktree yet.

Fix options: (1) claim-then-spawn with cwd=target worktree (loads target CLAUDE.md naturally); (2) agent reads the target's CLAUDE.md explicitly after cd. Also: workers inherit workspace/CLAUDE.md (CLU persona + RTK) = noise for a worker; the engine-vs-target convention boundary needs thought. Ties to zm5/productization + the agnostic rule.
outcome: done
shipped: GRID-054 target CLAUDE.md (merged)

PART 1 - migrate to in-process FakeStore (~20 calls, mechanical/safe): TestFileStep(6), TestClose(4), TestConfig(6), TestClaimArtifacts, TestFlowFromAgents, TestLogRender, TestWorklog.
PART 2 - speed up the must-stay process/git tests (the dominant cost - 13 make_store/bd-init calls): TestWorktree(13), TestNamedRepo(5), TestInitAndStoreGuard(6), TestSpawn(3), TestRun(4), TestSkeleton(3), TestPruneWorkers, TestWorktreeNoOrigin. Approach: share one store per class via setUpClass instead of per-test setUp; skip bd init where the test only needs git or argparse (TestSkeleton needs neither bd nor git).
Both parts needed to reach 'seconds'; Part 1 alone leaves the suite minutes-slow because Part 2 holds the bd-init cost.
CORRECTION after starting (2026-06): the grep-based Part1/Part2 split was misleading. The FakeStore behavioral migration was ALREADY largely done (TestModel/Claim/Flow/DoneBlock/ClaimArtifacts/Trace use call()+FakeStore). Part 1 actually delivered = dropped needless new_store()/bd-init from 4 non-bd classes (TestLogRender/FlowFromAgents/AgentFrontmatter/Config) - committed. REAL remaining work (the dominant cost) = the git/process tests that genuinely need bd+git: TestWorktree(13 make_store), TestNamedRepo, TestInitAndStoreGuard, TestSpawn, TestRun, TestClose. Approach: share ONE store/worktree-base per class via setUpClass; skip bd where only git or argparse is needed (TestSkeleton). That is the whole game for 'suite in seconds'.
PROGRESS: Part 1 (drop needless bd-init, af3e8c8) + Part 2a (template-copy new_store, 27d0481) shipped: suite 5.6min -> 3.8min, green.

Part 2b (the remaining cost, ~260s of the 3.8min): the worktree/git tests (TestWorktree x13, TestNamedRepo, TestClose) run ~20s EACH - NOT bd init (templated) but per-COMMAND Dolt startup (~2-3s) x several bd calls per run_tg. PROVEN fix: convert them to in-process call(cmd_*) with FakeStore (instant store ops) + a real make_repo git target (real worktree). Their assertions are on REAL git (branch feat/w, root branch unchanged, .worktrees dir, reuse) - keep those; only the store path moves to FakeStore. Est ~260s -> ~26s. setUp pattern: self.root=make_repo(parent,'engine'); write_steps; write_config(projects=parent,specs=root); GRID_ROOT_OVERRIDE=root; inject FakeStore. Then run_tg->call, bd_in->self.store/call(cmd_show). ~15 tests, careful (FakeStore must cover what cmd_claim/ensure_worktree need).
close_reason: B4 finished: suite 5.6min -> 55s (6x), green. Part1 (drop needless bd-init) + Part2a (template-copy new_store) + Part2b (worktree/run/file tests in-process FakeStore+real git; template-copy contract store). Residual ~30s = intentional real-bd contract; optional further cut = run a smaller real-bd subset.

title: Simplify the planning phase: collapse refine+develop+decompose+review into ONE session; drop the planner agent
notes:
Proposal (from the 2026-06 planning-phase review): the agent half (build->review->pr->merge) is a connected flow, but the planning half is manual driver glue with unproven value. Collapse capture -> [one session: refine + develop + decompose + review] -> build.

DROP the planner agent: the breakdown is design work and ALREADY lives in the spec's 'Build tasks' section; the planner only transcribed it + wrote child specs. Move decomposition into develop; the driver files the stories straight from the approved spec.

Wins: one review gate instead of two; no invisible develop->plan glue (driver hand-creating epic+brief+plan-task); fewer entry points.

Evidence the multi-step phase didn't earn its keep: planner first-run wired deps at task-level (bug); F1 overran scope; spec-location split (sae); brief-vs-spec double-naming; plan doc in /tmp (tba). Several of those edges vanish if the planner goes.

Open Qs when we return: do we still want child specs per story (or stories reference the main spec)? does 'refine into epics' stay, or do todos go straight into a develop session? what happens to the review-plan step (becomes the single develop gate)?
Concrete shape (from the spec experiment): spec-writing becomes an AGENT STEP, not a live co-design session - vanilla writes specs fast and asks no questions, so it runs autonomously. Flow: human PRIMES the epic (outcome/brief) + files the first story ('write the spec') -> spec-writer agent step writes the spec (guidance: phased + checkpoints, reviewer affordances like tables/mermaid, NO imposed format), commits it, opens a spec PR -> human reviews the spec PR in GitHub (inline comments, 33j) -> watcher pulls comments -> agent revises or human merges (= approved) -> build the phase-stories. Co-design goes ASYNC: prime + PR-review-loop replace the synchronous session (more attention-protecting; aligns with engagement-scheduler bk4). The spec-writer is a NEW agent step, unlike the removed planner (it writes, doesn't transcribe). develop stops being a human+driver skill; human-facing parts collapse to prime (before) + review-the-spec-PR (after). Needs: spec-writer step file w/ guidance; specs tracked in-repo (sae); spec-PR + comment-watcher (33j).
SPEC EXPERIMENT CONCLUDED (gcc cockpit, vanilla vs superpowers x plain/phased/affordances; files at ~/workspace/spec-experiment/). Findings: (1) imposing a spec FORMAT degrades it (house format thinned vanilla 430->226L); (2) light WORKFLOW guidance 'phases, each a story w/ review checkpoint' kept depth + mapped onto --blocked-by stories; (3) light REVIEWABILITY guidance 'use tables/diagrams as affordances' jumped reviewability w/o imposing format (vanilla 8->61 tables, +mermaid, +screen mockup - all signal); (4) vanilla benefits MOST from the 2 hints and is fast + asks no questions = viable as an autonomous AGENT STEP.
SETTLED spec-writer step prompt: 'Write a spec for the epic's outcome. Read the repo for accuracy. It must be clear for build/review agents and reviewable by a human. Break substantial work into phases, each its own story with a review checkpoint. Use reviewer affordances - tables, mermaid diagrams, a screen/layout mockup where relevant. No format beyond that. Commit it and open a spec PR.'
TO BUILD: the spec-writer agent step (this prompt); specs tracked in-repo (sae); spec-PR + comment-watcher (33j); human-facing planning collapses to prime-epic + review-spec-PR.
outcome: obsolete
stale: the planner agent + separate plan step were already removed (driver.md 'How work flows'); develop is now the single co-design session. 'Drop the planner' is done; 'collapse into one session' is the current flow.

title: Spec pre-review - an agent vets a draft spec for gaps before it reaches the human
description: An agent spec-reviewer that vets a DRAFT spec before it reaches the human's review-plan gate: is it falsifiable? are the acceptance criteria concrete and testable? any ambiguity, gap, or scope creep? Catch spec problems with an agent FIRST, so the human reviews a cleaned-up spec and fewer gaps slip through to cause rework downstream. Directly targets a pattern seen repeatedly this session: ambiguous/gappy specs causing rework rounds (moving specs; 'criterion could read two ways'; missing produce/route scoping). Distinct from the code reviewer (which vets BUILT code) - this vets the SPEC pre-build, in the develop->review-plan flow. The original 'reviewer' half of this item is covered by ongoing quality work (the reviewer itself, bui workflow-validation, k4i judgement quality); the novel, unaddressed kernel is spec pre-review. Ties to the driver 'prime every review' discipline and protecting the human's attention.
notes:
Outcome: the reviewer (plus a spec pre-reviewer) catches the classes of issue that currently slip to the human, so human attention goes to genuine judgment, not catching misses. The reviewer is our confirmed weak link - four human-catches this session the agent reviewer missed (plan-add agnosticism, stale-base, mine naming-debt, provenance-overclaim). Fed by 1A's retro, which surfaces WHERE the reviewer is weak.

Consolidated from (refinement):
- jm2: systematize human-catches into durable coder/reviewer checks (every catch becomes a sharpening).
- a1z: reviewer-vs-Copilot gap - benchmark and close the reviewer's blind spots.
- mnp: spec-reviewer step - pre-review specs before the human's review-plan gate.

title: Feedback loop closes itself - every step reports feedback that aggregates into proposed sharpenings
notes:
Outcome: every step reports structured feedback, it stays clean, and it aggregates into a digest that PROPOSES concrete sharpenings - so improving the system is driven by its own signals, not log archaeology. Shape: capture -> clean -> aggregate+propose.

First slice already building: dxd / GRID-012 (tg reflect --friction + retro Friction section + reviewer reflect step). This epic broadens it.

Consolidated from (refinement):
- 94v: broaden reflection beyond the spec - what went well + friction; make automated feedback core to every step.
- 8m9: tg reflect upsert per task (not append) - duplicate reflections skew the retro.
- 89k: retro step that proposes improvements to the agents (the 'propose' half the loop is missing today).
MVP delivered (613f5d4): the CAPTURE half - single freeform tg reflect --feedback; coder AND reviewer report (reviewer gained a reflect step it never had); tg retro surfaces the raw feedback text for a human/LLM to read. This is 94v done the minimal way (freeform, no codified channels/counts). REMAINING: 89k (an analyser agent that reads the retro feedback + proposes coder/reviewer/spec sharpenings - the 'closes itself' part); 8m9 (reflect dedup - now low priority since feedback is freeform text, not counted).
Design spec (for review): specs/GRID-019-feedback-loop-closes-itself.md - refines this epic into 3 stories: (1) tg retro at any scope, (2) retro as epic-close ceremony, (3) cadence-driven trend analyser -> inbox.
close_reason: Feedback loop shipped: tg retro any-scope (story 1), the epic-close ceremony 2a-2d (story 2), the auditor on a cadence (story 3). The loop can now close itself - and this close is the first exercise of it.

title: engagement scheduler (organise the human's attention/time)
notes: THE big one - designed in depth this session, never captured (BACKLOG.md 'Planner'). A driver capability that plans the HUMAN's engagement, not just sorts tasks - the human is the scarce resource, NOT a uniform worker (energy, motivation, time vary; they set the objective per session). Inputs: a declared session MODE (push-a-goal / refine / queue-and-leave / review-session / low-energy) + capacity, plus pipeline state. Insight: human time is lumpy+scarce, pool time abundant+async -> spend synchronous presence to unblock the MOST async work (front-load develop+approve), review outputs in bursts; reason about runway. Touch priority: unblock > drain (review/merge) > prefetch (develop). OFFLINE MODE: when away, the pool runs BOUNDED autonomous work (security upgrades, dep bumps, draft specs, architecture reports) batched for the human's return - autonomous at front, human-gated at back, opt-in by class; trap = the review-burden boomerang (rate-limit to review bandwidth + triage on return). Calendar is one optional availability source. The ATTENTION/SCHEDULE planner, distinct from GRID-004's DECOMPOSE planner (two-planner name clash). Subsumes smart-inbox-triage.

title: pr-watcher/reviewer must read CI accurately
description: watch-pr (and the reviewer) must read CI accurately - do not conclude ci-failed when CI is actually green/pending. Live evidence 2026-07-03: after the GRID-043 resolver force-pushed a rebased #90 (j2l), watch-pr concluded ci-failed and bounced j2l back to build - but #90's CI was ALL-GREEN (both workflow runs SUCCESS, no failure in history). Mechanism: a force-push CANCELS the old in-flight CI run and starts a fresh one; watch-pr read the superseded/cancelled run (or caught the new run still pending) and scored it as a failure. Fix: watch-pr must read the LATEST run for the CURRENT head commit (HEAD SHA), treat a cancelled/superseded run as not-a-failure, and on a pending check poll-then-block rather than ever concluding ci-failed on pending (as watch-pr.md already says). Cost: an unnecessary build->review->open-pr->watch-pr cycle per misread, and a risk of a build<->watch-pr loop. Especially likely now that the resolver force-pushes rebased branches, so this pairs with GRID-043.
notes:
Live, the pr-watcher misdiagnosed a CI failure (blamed Docker/network; it was a unit-test failure). Prompts now say fetch the actual failing job/logs before concluding - guidance, not enforced; verify it holds in a live run with CI. From BACKLOG.md.
Concrete instance (B3/8fm.5.4): watch-pr ran 25+ min stuck on a PR with NO CI configured (checks=0, MERGEABLE). watch-pr.md tells the agent to verify 'CI green' but gives no exit for the no-CI case, so it waits forever for checks that never appear. Driver had to kill it and finish the step by hand. Fix: watch-pr must treat 'no checks configured' as pass-through (-> done). Also a clean dxd friction fixture (this should surface in a retro, not need a kill).

title: handle subscription usage limits gracefully
description: Session/usage-limit circuit breaker. Today: no handling - when the Claude session limit hits ('You've hit your session limit - resets 9:30pm (Europe/London)'), each claude -p worker fails instantly and exits; the loop keeps ticking and RESPAWNS into the limit while sweep resets the orphaned tasks -> a churn loop (spawn->fail->reset->respawn) that thrashes bd and spams logs until reset. Design: (1) DETECT - scan an exited worker's log for the session-limit message; (2) OPEN on first receipt - stop spawning AND kill all live workers (they'll only fail); (3) PARSE the reset time from the message and hold the breaker open until then; (4) HALF-OPEN CLOSE - after the reset, probe with ONE worker: success -> resume, still-limited -> re-open + re-parse the new reset; (5) breaker STATE (open + reset-time) is a small state file / store-derived so a loop RESTART respects an open breaker rather than charging back in. Immediate manual workaround: Ctrl-C tg run on hitting the limit; restart after reset. Ties to c1y (kill orphaned workers - the breaker reuses the kill path), tp1/i5x (restart-safe state).
notes:
A worker that hits the Claude usage limit exits without tg done; sweep respawns it -> hits limit again -> respawn loop, no detection, one shared quota blocks everyone. Detect the limit signal in the stream-json log, PAUSE THE WHOLE LOOP, surface 'blocked: usage limit, resets <time>' to the inbox, schedule resume at reset (claude --resume replays context). From BACKLOG.md.

title: backlog refinement: shape rough todos into scoped epics (coherent outcomes)
notes: An EPIC is a coherent OUTCOME, not a promoted 1:1 todo and not about size - some epics are one big thing, some are several related small things (e.g. dja+3dd+bcd+dg2 = one 'lifecycle accuracy/lineage' outcome). REFINEMENT is the deliberate process that turns rough, cheap, overlapping backlog captures into scoped epics, consolidating related items (or splitting big ones), deciding in/out. This is the 'WHAT/WHY refinement (Quorra)' backlog item, realized. Layered lifecycle: capture (rough todos) -> REFINE (todos -> scoped epics) -> PLAN (planner GRID-004: epic -> child stories) -> BUILD (story -> review -> PR). KEY BENEFIT: active epics = current focus areas = the prioritisation signal a flat todo list can't give. Also fixes the dg2 lineage gap: the epic is the connective tissue (epic -> stories -> PRs; epic records which backlog items it addresses), so 'this shipped thing came from that idea' becomes structural. GUARDRAIL: epics are OPTIONAL - a genuinely small self-contained fix skips the epic and takes the light story-only path; epics are for coherent outcomes worth scoping/grouping, not a mandatory wrapper. Supersedes/refines the Quorra item in BACKLOG.md.

title: lifecycle lineage: link backlog item -> spec -> story (currently disconnected)
notes: Verified: a developed backlog item (e.g. qiq) gets CLOSED but the resulting build story (e.g. 41j) has no parent/dep back to it - they are joined only by the shared spec FILE, not in the bead graph. So you cannot trace 'this shipped story grew from that idea'. The develop->review chain and the tg-file build story are two islands. Want: the lifecycle connected end to end - develop produces (or links) the story, the originating backlog item closes WITH a pointer to the story, so lineage is recorded. Same family as the develop->review-plan handoff gap (standalone tasks not under a story; spec artifact not travelling). Handling has also been inconsistent (sometimes a backlog item is developed, sometimes a fresh review-plan task is made, the build story is always filed separately).

The agent ready/queue view should show only actionable agent tasks (for:<role>) and exclude containers (stories/epics with no role). title: tg status mislabels dependency-blocked tasks as queue/ready
notes: Found live: a task with an open 'blocks' dependency (e.g. tg file --blocked-by) is correctly excluded from bd ready (the pool skips it), but tg status shows it in 'queue' and 'blocked (0)'. Cause: task_from_bead's status projection only checks for:role/assignee/closed - it never inspects dependencies. The bead from 'bd list --json' DOES carry dependency_count + dependencies, so the fix is: in core/tasks.task_from_bead (or bucket), mark status 'blocked' when the task has an open blocker dep. Behaviour is correct (pool uses bd ready); only the human display is wrong - which undermines trust in the dependency-chain automation. Pairs with the agent/human inspection-command reviews.

title: spec-reviewer step: pre-review the spec before the human's review-plan gate
notes: A spec-stage analog of the code reviewer. Sits develop -> spec-review (agent) -> review-plan (human, primed) -> build. ADVISE not gate: it surfaces concerns at the human's gate (does NOT auto-reject - the human carries intent and a flag may be a deliberate choice). It is what AUTO-GENERATES the 'Review focus' we've been hand-writing. Would have caught plan-add: applying the agnostic check (AGENTS.md) to the spec's DECISIONS table flags 'tg plan-add adds a workflow command to an agnostic engine - violation?' at spec-time (cheap) vs build-time (a wasted cycle). Checks: design traps (agnosticism/layering), gaps/undefined terms, internal contradictions, unfalsifiable decisions, conformance to the plan-format. Agnostic: reads the project's spec conventions (plan-format guide, AGENTS principles), not hardcoded rules. RISK: noise - must flag REAL things sharply and PRIME attention, not flood (the review-burden asymmetry). Related: code reviewer, GRID-005 reflection/retro (was the flag right? = feedback).

title: review-plan: short daily summary of completed work
GitHub as the review surface: review specs/PRs via comments, a watcher pulls the feedback back in
notes:
GitHub is already central (code + PRs). Idea: do the actual review THERE - PR (and maybe spec) review as GitHub comments - and a watcher process ingests that feedback back into the-grid, routing it (PR comments -> a rejection's notes -> changes -> build, via GRID-008 notes-forward). Replaces terminal tg close/tg done with real GitHub review UI. Connects external-triggers (the watcher) + notes-forward (GRID-008) + the gates. Open Q: specs are files, not PRs - how to review them on GitHub? Maybe a lightweight PR per spec.
Concrete mechanism (decided): spec review = a 'spec PR' in GitHub. develop writes the spec, commits it on a branch, opens a PR; the human reviews with native inline line comments (the 'highlight + comment' viewer, no tool to build); a watcher pulls the PR comments back into tg as feedback; the agent revises (push) or the human approves; merging the spec PR IS the approval, then build the stories. Mirror of the code-review PR flow - specs get the same surface as code. GitHub renders markdown tables + mermaid inline, so reviewer affordances (tables/diagrams) land in the review surface. REQUIREMENT/opens sae: specs need a tracked git home with a remote - natural home is the TARGET repo's specs/ (tracked, currently gitignored), so the spec PR sits with the code it specifies. Ties to gia (scope boundary) and 3z2 (the review step = open spec PR + feedback watcher, not a tg gate).

title: backup mechanisms for the-grid data (bd store, specs, reflections/signals)
notes:
The data is getting valuable: the bd store (.beads), the specs, and accumulating reflections/signals. Need a backup mechanism + cadence before it grows. Ken's standing rule: back up before any migration. Consider regular bd export (JSONL) + the .beads dolt history, plus specs already in the workspace git repo.
DURABILITY FINDINGS (from the one-time de-risk): bd data lives in gitignored Dolt (embeddeddolt/). .beads/issues.jsonl is EXPLICITLY gitignored (root .gitignore:14) - bd treats JSONL exports as local-only. bd's INTENDED durability is Dolt remotes (bd dolt push) or bd backup init/sync (filesystem or DoltHub), NOT JSONL-in-git. bd hooks install (--shared -> committable .beads-hooks/) auto-exports/syncs on commit/push. PROPER ge0 MECHANISM: likely a the-grid-data git/Dolt remote or DoltHub (mirrors the horde-data nested-repo pattern from memory), + bd hooks for auto-export, + a cadence. STOPGAP NOW: bd-issues-snapshot.jsonl committed to origin - point-in-time, goes stale; re-run before any structural change until the real mechanism ships.

title: retro step: periodic system-health review that proposes improvements to the agents
notes:
A SYSTEM-level retro (distinct from GRID-005's per-epic tg retro, which refines the spec template). Reads operational health - reflections, objective signals, blocks, rework rates, friction events (e.g. the pytest loop), cycle times - finds RECURRING friction, and proposes EVIDENCE-BACKED improvements to the steps/AGENTS/conventions ('coders defaulted to pytest in 3/3 builds -> this fix'). Analysis is automatable; the decision is human-gated (a bad step edit degrades all future work). Natural home for accumulated dogfood findings. Completes the stack: build -> reflect(task) -> retro(epic/template) -> retro(system/agents).
Routing heuristic for proposed improvements - classify by SCOPE: one task -> the spec's review-focus; any repo/stack (generic agent competence) -> the step file; this project (stack/layout/conventions) -> the project AGENTS.md; all my projects (personal style) -> global CLAUDE.md. Sharp discriminator between step-file and AGENTS.md is the AGNOSTICISM TEST: 'would this apply to a frontend/Go repo?' yes->step, no->AGENTS. The retro proposes the change AND its target, justified by the test - which makes it self-guarding against baking project-specifics into agnostic steps.
DESIGN DETAIL (from discussion): Assembled from 3 sources aggregated over a time range, seeking RECURRING patterns: (1) reflections, (2) objective signals from bd history (span all steps), (3) log-derived friction (the pytest loop was invisible to reflections but plain in logs - so the retro MUST read logs, not just self-reports). Agent coverage: signals span all steps; reflections coder-only until 94v broadens them. DISPOSITION TRACKING (the key, to avoid re-litigating fixed things): a finding IS a bead with a disposition - addressed (closed, LINKED to the fix) or wont-fix (closed, with reason). The retro suppresses addressed/wontfix findings UNLESS data SINCE the fix date shows recurrence - in which case it escalates 'still happening despite fix Y' rather than re-proposing. The fix-link is bidirectional: later you can trace a convention (e.g. the AGENTS pytest guard) back to its motivating finding, so you don't revert a working fix. This is the provenance/settled-facts ledger (proposed -> addressed/wontfix -> superseded) scoped to findings. STAYS ON-CORE: findings are beads, the retro is a step/agent - no new tg primitives; tg just exposes the raw data.
FINDING (reviewer miss #2): the reviewer passed PR #5's tg plan-add despite AGENTS.md's agnostic rule ('do not bake project-specific assumptions into tg'). plan-add hardcoded a 'build' step + required a 'spec' artifact + was a planning-specific command in a generic-primitives tool. Human caught it, agent reviewer didn't (same pattern as GRID-005 architecture). The agnostic rule needs to be a SHARP, concrete reviewer check: 'reject any hardcoded step/role/workflow name or required named artifact in tg/core - workflow conventions belong in agent markdown, not the engine'. Candidate reviewer.md enrichment.
